title: Plantar fasciitis release fascia anatomy, Baxter nerve, release limits (1/3-2/3), arch risks
summary: Medial 1/3-2/3 release; protect Baxter's (lat plantar 1st branch); avoid complete (arch collapse). Gastroc recession adjunct.
must_know: Plantar fascia: thick aponeurosis with central (strongest, to 2-4 toes), medial (to hallux), lateral bands. Origin from medial calcaneal tuberosity (medial and anterior aspects); inserts via digital slips to proximal phalanges bases and plantar plates. Windlass mechanism: dorsiflexion of MTPs tightens fascia, elevates longitudinal arch and stabilizes midfoot for push-off. Also supports medial arch (secondary to spring ligament).

Release: open or endoscopic. Incision medial or plantar. Release only medial 1/3 to 2/3 of fascia origin (preserve lateral band to maintain arch stability and prevent lateral column overload/dorsolateral midfoot pain). May release deep fascia of abductor hallucis if Baxter's nerve entrapment suspected. Avoid complete release.

Baxter's nerve (first branch of lateral plantar nerve): provides motor to abductor digiti minimi and sensation to plantar lateral foot/lateral 4th and 5th toes. Courses anterior (deep) to medial calcaneal tuberosity between quadratus plantae and flexor digitorum brevis; vulnerable during release. Consider simultaneous release or decompression if symptoms.

Risks/complications: lateral plantar nerve injury (Baxter's), complete fascia release leading to arch collapse/flatfoot, lateral column overload, dorsolateral midfoot pain, heel pad atrophy (especially post-injection), plantar fascia rupture (risk higher in athletes, minimalist runners, or after corticosteroid), recurrence, wound issues, nerve entrapment. Gastrocnemius recession is alternative for equinus contribution (limited ankle dorsiflexion exacerbates strain).
nerve_course: Lateral plantar/Baxter's anterior medial tuberosity QP/FDB
common_pimp_questions: In plantar fasciitis release, why limit to medial 1/3-2/3 and what nerve (Baxter's) courses anterior to the medial calcaneal tuberosity between QP and FDB?
Describe the windlass mechanism of the plantar fascia and the consequence of complete (vs partial) release on the longitudinal arch and lateral column.
What are the 3 bands of the plantar fascia and their insertions, and why is the central band most relevant to release for fasciitis?
When is simultaneous Baxter's nerve release or gastrocnemius recession indicated in plantar fasciitis surgery, and what anatomy supports this?
What are the risks of complete plantar fascia release or prior corticosteroid injection in athletes or minimalist runners?

title: Cervical laminectomy decompression (posterior note), C5 palsy, lateral mass
summary: Midline posterior subperiost lamina/flavum lat to dura; lat mass for fusion. C5 palsy risk. Anterior strong (fascia/RLN/symp from 12001).
must_know: Anterior approach (for ACDF or anterior decompression): 3 fascial layers (superficial investing with platysma/EJV; pretracheal continuous with carotid sheath containing superior/inferior thyroid vessels; prevertebral thick, lining prevertebral muscles with cervical sympathetic trunk on surface). Landmarks: angle of mandible ~C2-3 level, carotid tubercle at C6. Superficial to deep: platysma (facial n. VII), anterior border of SCM (spinal accessory XI, retract lateral), strap muscles (C1-3, retract medial), longus colli (segmental cervical nerves). Deep: split longus colli and anterior longitudinal ligament in midline, subperiosteal exposure, retract longus/ALL laterally, confirm level with needle/localizer under fluoro. Risks: recurrent laryngeal nerve (~2.3%, right more variable/vulnerable, protect by medial longus colli retractor), sympathetic chain on lateral longus colli at C6 (injury causes ipsilateral Horner syndrome: ptosis, miosis, anhidrosis), carotid sheath contents, retropharyngeal hematoma (airway emergency).

Posterior approach (for laminectomy +/- fusion): midline incision, subperiosteal dissection to expose lamina and facets. Identify and protect dura/cord. Remove ligamentum flavum (attaches halfway up undersurface of lamina); stay lateral to dura with blunt dissection. For foraminotomy or lateral mass fixation, expose lateral masses. Landmarks: spinous processes (count levels), facets, lateral masses (for screws: start at midpoint, trajectory ~30° lateral, 15° cephalad). C5 palsy risk (deltoid/biceps weakness, often transient) associated with posterior decompression (foraminotomy extent, cord drift, reperfusion). Risks: cord or nerve root injury, C5 palsy, vertebral artery (far lateral), infection, post-laminectomy kyphosis if fusion not done.
common_pimp_questions: Name the three fascial layers encountered in the anterior cervical approach and key contents of the pretracheal layer; what is the RLN injury rate and laterality preference?
What is the C5 palsy risk in posterior cervical laminectomy, and what maneuvers or limits mitigate it during foraminotomy or decompression?
During anterior cervical exposure, what landmarks correspond to C2-3 and C6 levels, and how does the longus colli plane guide deep dissection and sympathetic chain protection?
Describe the planes from platysma to longus colli in the anterior cervical approach (innervation of each muscle layer) and the deep step after splitting longus colli and ALL.
Which structures are at risk with lateral retractor placement or over-dissection in anterior cervical surgery, and how is the sympathetic chain protected on the longus colli at C6?
notes: v1_4 new/enriched v3 cervical (anterior strong, posterior limited).
quality_gaps: Posterior full layered/C5 mechanism needs better page
